target.title: fix: carry reply metadata into runtime context
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a first-class current-turn reply metadata payload to `CurrentInboundPromptContext` so Telegram/forum reply facts survive the reply prompt/runtime handoff as structured OpenClaw metadata.

This is the Morty patch shape, polished for upstream:

- separates `replyTargetPresent` from `quotePresent`, so a normal replied-to message body is not mislabeled as a selected quote
- preserves reply metadata even when the text context prefix is empty
- keeps replied-to body text out of the trusted metadata block and verbose trace
- folds in the hidden runtime-context routing from #89428 so current inbound/reply metadata no longer gets prepended into `promptForSession` / `promptForModel`

## Related

Addresses the runtime handoff portion of #88032.

Builds on the same routing direction as #89428. This branch includes the equivalent hidden-runtime-context behavior so the reply-specific contract can be reviewed as one coherent patch.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** A Telegram/forum reply can carry `ReplyToId` / `ReplyChain` facts in channel context, but the agent runtime needs a structured current-turn reply contract that stays available to the model without being persisted as user-authored prompt text.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout from this PR branch on Linux, running the real TypeScript modules through `node --import tsx`. The proof uses redacted Telegram/forum-style ids.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** First, built a finalized Telegram group/forum-style inbound context with current message id `34974`, topic id `777`, reply target id `34971`, and one reply-chain entry; passed that context through the real `buildReplyPromptEnvelope(...)` prompt-prelude path; rendered the current-turn runtime prefix with `buildCurrentInboundPromptContextPrefix(...)`; checked that structured reply metadata appears before untrusted context and that the trusted reply metadata block does not include the replied-to body text.

```bash
PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/root/projects/openclaw/node_modules node --import tsx -e 'const { finalizeInboundContext } = await import("./src/auto-reply/reply/inbound-context.ts"); const { buildReplyPromptEnvelope } = await import("./src/auto-reply/reply/prompt-prelude.ts"); const { buildCurrentInboundPromptContextPrefix } = await import("./src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/runtime-context-prompt.ts"); const sessionCtx = finalizeInboundContext({ Body: "Sean, answer this old reply", BodyStripped: "Sean, answer this old reply", Provider: "telegram", Surface: "telegram", OriginatingChannel: "telegram", ChatType: "group", MessageSid: "34974", MessageThreadId: 777, ReplyToId: "34971", ReplyToBody: "old target body", ReplyToSender: "Alice", ReplyChain: [{ messageId: "34971", threadId: "777", sender: "Alice", body: "old target body" }] }); const envelope = buildReplyPromptEnvelope({ ctx: sessionCtx, sessionCtx, baseBody: "Sean, answer this old reply", prefixedBody: "Sean, answer this old reply", hasUserBody: true, inboundUserContext: "Conversation info (untrusted metadata):\nreply_to_id=34971", isBareSessionReset: false, startupAction: "new" }); const prefix = buildCurrentInboundPromptContextPrefix(envelope.currentInboundContext); console.log(JSON.stringify({ reply: envelope.currentInboundContext?.reply, text: envelope.currentInboundContext?.text, metadataBeforeUntrusted: prefix.indexOf("Current reply metadata") >= 0 && prefix.indexOf("Current reply metadata") < prefix.indexOf("Conversation info"), metadataLeaksReplyBody: prefix.slice(0, prefix.indexOf("Conversation info")).includes("old target body") }, null, 2));'
```

Then, exercised the hidden runtime-context routing with the real `resolveRuntimeContextPromptParts(...)` helper and verified that reply metadata stays out of the submitted/persisted prompt strings while remaining in `runtimeContext`.

```bash
node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'PROOF'
import { resolveRuntimeContextPromptParts } from './src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/runtime-context-prompt.ts';
const parts = resolveRuntimeContextPromptParts({
  effectivePrompt: 'what is this referring to?',
  transcriptPrompt: 'what is this referring to?',
  currentInboundContext: {
    text: 'Conversation info (untrusted metadata):\nreply_to_id=redacted-34971',
    reply: {
      currentMessageId: 'redacted-34974',
      replyToId: 'redacted-34971',
      replyTargetPresent: true,
      quotePresent: false,
      replyChainPresent: true,
      replyChainMessageIds: ['redacted-34971'],
    },
  },
});
console.log(JSON.stringify({
  prompt: parts.prompt,
  modelPrompt: parts.modelPrompt ?? parts.prompt,
  promptContainsReplyMetadata: parts.prompt.includes('Current reply metadata') || parts.prompt.includes('redacted-34971'),
  modelPromptContainsReplyMetadata: (parts.modelPrompt ?? parts.prompt).includes('Current reply metadata') || (parts.modelPrompt ?? parts.prompt).includes('redacted-34971'),
  runtimeContextContainsReplyMetadata: Boolean(parts.runtimeContext?.includes('Current reply metadata')),
  runtimeContextContainsReplyToId: Boolean(parts.runtimeContext?.includes('redacted-34971')),
}, null, 2));
PROOF
```

**Evidence after fix:** Console output from the first real module command:

```json
{
  "reply": {
    "currentMessageId": "34974",
    "threadId": "777",
    "replyToId": "34971",
    "replyTargetPresent": true,
    "quotePresent": false,
    "replyChainPresent": true,
    "replyChainMessageIds": ["34971"]
  },
  "text": "Conversation info (untrusted metadata):\nreply_to_id=34971",
  "metadataBeforeUntrusted": true,
  "metadataLeaksReplyBody": false
}
```

Console output from the hidden runtime-context proof:

```json
{
  "prompt": "what is this referring to?",
  "modelPrompt": "what is this referring to?",
  "promptContainsReplyMetadata": false,
  "modelPromptContainsReplyMetadata": false,
  "runtimeContextContainsReplyMetadata": true,
  "runtimeContextContainsReplyToId": true
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** Reply target metadata is structured, rendered before untrusted context, omitted from the visible/model prompt strings, and delivered through hidden runtime context. The trusted reply metadata block does not leak the replied-to body text.


**Live Telegram/forum transport proof (redacted):** A real Telegram forum reply was sent in an existing group topic on 2026-06-05 UTC. No live OpenClaw install or runtime was changed for this transport proof.

Redacted inbound metadata supplied to the agent turn:

```json
{
  "channel": "telegram",
  "surface": "telegram",
  "chat_type": "group",
  "is_forum": true,
  "topic_id": "<redacted-topic>",
  "message_id": "<redacted-current>",
  "reply_to_id": "<redacted-anchor>",
  "was_mentioned": true,
  "has_reply_context": true
}
```

The assembled current message included a Telegram reply target for the proof anchor while the persisted session user message remained the user-authored text only:

```json
{
  "role": "user",
  "sourceChannel": "telegram",
  "content": "@crustaSean_bot PROOF_REPLY_90745"
}
```

Then the PR branch was exercised with the same redacted Telegram/forum shape through the real `resolveRuntimeContextPromptParts(...)` helper:

```json
{
  "prompt": "@crustaSean_bot PROOF_REPLY_90745",
  "modelPrompt": "@crustaSean_bot PROOF_REPLY_90745",
  "promptContainsReplyMetadata": false,
  "modelPromptContainsReplyMetadata": false,
  "runtimeContextContainsReplyMetadata": true,
  "runtimeContextContainsReplyToId": true,
  "runtimeContextContainsForumSignal": true
}
```

This pairs a real Telegram/forum reply event with the PR-branch runtime handoff: the transport supplies `reply_to_id`/forum/mention facts, the session prompt remains clean user text, and the PR branch keeps reply/forum metadata in hidden runtime context instead of visible/model prompt text.

**What was not tested:** A live Telegram bot round-trip against this PR branch, because Sean's live OpenClaw runtime was intentionally not changed.

## Tests

- `PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/root/projects/openclaw/node_modules node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/runtime-context-prompt.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/prompt-prelude.test.ts` - passed 2 Vitest shards, 36 tests
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD` - passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/runtime-context-prompt.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/prompt-prelude.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts` - passed on 2026-06-15 after the embedded-attempt handoff repair and rebase onto current main; 3 Vitest shards, 98 tests
- `git diff --check` - passed on 2026-06-15

target.title: fix(memory-core): guard searches during safe reindex
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Fixes an intermittent memory search/status race where safe full reindex temporarily points the manager at a half-built temp DB, allowing readers to see chunks without matching index metadata and fail closed with `index metadata is missing`.
- Adds a reindex latch so search waits for in-flight full reindex work before reading index identity, FTS availability, or query state.
- Keeps status reads stable during the temp-DB window by serving the last durable status snapshot.
- Rewrites the verified metadata on the canonical DB after the safe swap so the serialized-meta cache from the temp DB cannot skip the durable write.

Why does this matter now?

- #90361 reports intermittent built-in memory search failures even though the durable index is valid. The failure mode is timing-dependent, so a focused race guard is needed rather than only metadata recovery.

What is the intended outcome?

- Searches and status reads no longer observe the half-built temp DB during safe reindex.
- Immediate post-reindex reads see valid index identity metadata on the canonical DB.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- Remote embedding provider stress testing.
- Broader recovery for previously corrupted or stale on-disk memory stores.
- Changes to memory config, provider selection, or migration behavior.

What does success look like?

- Forced reindex, status, and search on a real CLI setup complete without `index metadata is missing`.
- Regression tests cover concurrent status/search reads, the provider-initialization await gap, and a reader that is already active when full reindex reaches the DB swap.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Whether the reindex latch is drained before every search path that reads index identity, FTS availability, or query data.
- Whether the stable status snapshot is limited to the safe reindex temp-DB window.
- Whether the post-swap metadata rewrite is idempotent and scoped to full safe reindex.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #90361

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #90422
Related #90395

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- No direct maintainer request; this is a contributor fix for #90361.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: built-in memory search/status should not fail with `index metadata is missing` after a safe forced reindex when the durable OpenClaw index is valid.
- Real environment tested: local source checkout at `0a9c4d1ef5`, Node `v24.15.0`, real OpenClaw CLI memory commands, temporary `OPENCLAW_HOME`, temporary workspace, FTS-only memory provider (`provider: "none"`, `model: "fts-only"`) to avoid external credentials.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
export OPENCLAW_HOME=<tmp-home>
export OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<tmp-config>/openclaw.json
export OPENCLAW_BUILD_ALL_NO_PNPM=1
export OPENCLAW_RUN_NODE_SKIP_DTS_BUILD=1
export CI=true
node scripts/run-node.mjs memory index --force --agent main --verbose
node scripts/run-node.mjs memory status --deep --index --agent main --json
node scripts/run-node.mjs memory search --agent main --query 'durable index identity' --max-results 5 --json
```

Temporary config used for the proof:

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "workspace": "<tmp-workspace>",
      "memorySearch": {
        "provider": "none",
        "model": "fts-only",
        "cache": { "enabled": false },
        "sync": { "watch": false, "onSessionStart": false, "onSearch": false }
      }
    },
    "list": [{ "id": "main", "default": true }]
  }
}
```

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
## Real OpenClaw CLI proof for #90361
repo_commit=0a9c4d1ef5
node=v24.15.0

### Force rebuild index
Memory Index (main)
Provider: none (requested: none)
Model: fts-only
Sources: memory (MEMORY.md + <tmp-path>)

[memory] sync: indexing memory files
Memory index updated (main).

### Parsed proof summary
status_files=1
status_chunks=1
status_dirty=false
status_index_identity=valid
status_has_missing_metadata=false
search_result_count=1
search_has_missing_metadata=false
search_contains_durable=true
search_first_path=MEMORY.md
search_first_snippet="Alpha topic about durable index identity.\n\nThis note proves memory search can recall the durable OpenClaw index after safe reindex.\n"
```

- Observed result after fix: forced CLI reindex completed, status reported a valid index identity with two indexed chunks, and CLI search returned a `MEMORY.md` result. Neither status nor search output contained `index metadata is missing`.
- What was not tested: live gateway tool invocation, remote/OpenAI embedding provider, concurrent stress across separate OS processes, cross-OS proof.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the real behavior proof uses the real CLI and SQLite/FTS memory path, but intentionally uses FTS-only provider mode so no external API key or embedding service is required. Provider/vector concurrency is covered by focused regression tests, not by live remote-provider proof.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the bug is timing-dependent; this PR adds regression coverage that forces searches/status reads into the safe reindex temp-DB window and the provider-initialization await window.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```bash
git diff --check
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.reindex-read-race.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/scripts/test-projects.test.ts
pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-sync-ops.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.reindex-read-race.test.ts
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main
```

Real CLI proof commands are listed in the Real behavior proof section.

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.reindex-read-race.test.ts`.
- Covers `status()` during in-flight full safe reindex.
- Covers `search()` waiting during the temp-DB window.
- Covers `search()` waiting when provider initialization yields and another actor starts a full safe reindex before identity/FTS reads.
- Covers full safe reindex waiting for an already-active index reader before swapping the DB.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The race allowed search/status readers to observe the half-built temp DB during safe reindex and fail closed with invalid or missing index identity metadata.

If no test was added, why not?

- Tests were added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Memory search/status now wait through an in-flight full safe reindex instead of reading transient temp-DB state.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

- Search can now wait for an in-flight full reindex before reading index identity or FTS state.
- Full safe reindex writes verified metadata again after reopening the canonical DB.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The latch is refcounted and cleared in `finally`.
- Active index readers are counted while DB reads run, and safe full reindex waits for them before swapping/closing the canonical DB.
- Status uses only a captured stable snapshot while the safe reindex temp DB is active.
- The post-swap metadata rewrite uses the same `nextMeta` built and verified during the full reindex.
- Focused regression tests cover the search/status race windows.
- Real CLI proof covers forced reindex, status, and search after the patch.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Ready for maintainer review and CI.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- CI needs to run on the PR.
- Maintainers may request additional live remote-provider stress proof if they want coverage beyond the local CLI/FTS proof and focused regression tests.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Fresh local autoreview completed cleanly: `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main` reported `autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported`.

target.title: feat(Signal): support note-to-self ingress
target.body:
## Summary

### What problem does this PR solve?

- Adds explicit Signal `ingressMode: "note-to-self"` support so linked-device `signal-cli` setups can use the operator's Signal Note to Self chat as the OpenClaw conversation surface.
- Removes the practical need to buy, register, and maintain a second phone number and Signal account just to run OpenClaw as a personal Signal bot.
- Promotes only self-addressed `syncMessage.sentMessage` envelopes into the normal inbound pipeline, while continuing to drop sent transcripts addressed to other people and direct own-account non-sync echoes.
- Adds self-reply echo suppression so OpenClaw replies sent into Note to Self do not re-enter as fresh prompts.

### Why does this matter now?

- Today, a reliable Signal setup effectively assumes a dedicated bot number/account. That is a high-friction requirement for the common personal use case where the operator only wants to message their own OpenClaw instance through Signal, not expose OpenClaw to contacts.
- Signal Note to Self is the natural secure chat surface for that use case, and linked-device Note to Self messages arrive as sent-sync events that need explicit, self-only handling before agent dispatch.
- This PR makes that behavior explicit and covered across opt-in config, UUID handling, attachments, edits, approval reactions, agent reaction actions, and self-reply loop protection.

### What is the intended outcome?

- Existing `standard` Signal deployments keep their current behavior.
- Users who opt into `channels.signal.ingressMode="note-to-self"` can send text, attachments, edits, and approval reactions through their own Signal Note to Self chat.
- OpenClaw ignores sent-sync transcripts to other Signal recipients and avoids looping on its own Note to Self replies.
- After community testing and follow-up hardening, this mode could become the default-recommended Signal gateway setup for personal deployments in a future PR.

### What is intentionally out of scope?

- No global/core `dmPolicy` changes.
- No change to the default-recommended Signal setup in this PR.
- No required `accountUuid` config for normal users; UUID discovery reads the local signal-cli account store instead.
- No durable persistence for Note to Self self-reply echo markers across gateway restarts; replay suppression is intentionally process-local.

### What does success look like?

- Focused Signal tests pass for account discovery, Note to Self sync promotion, UUID-only routing, edits, attachment-only messages, approval reactions, echo suppression, and legacy approval-reaction key compatibility.
- A real linked-device Signal setup can process phone-originated Note to Self text, image-only, edited-message, approval-reaction, and agent-driven reaction action flows without observed self-reply loops.

### What should reviewers focus on?

- Whether `ingressMode="note-to-self"` is bounded to Signal and remains opt-in.
- Whether self-addressed sent-sync promotion cannot accidentally process transcripts to other recipients.
- Whether echo suppression is strong enough without adding persistent state.
- Whether UUID normalization/discovery and approval reaction matching preserve existing behavior.

## Linked context

### Which issue does this close?

Closes #75870.

### Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Supersedes and extends #75890.

Related #5722.

### Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No direct maintainer request cited; this follows the linked issue and earlier PR context.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Linked-device Signal deployments can route user-authored Note to Self messages into OpenClaw. The same mode must keep ignoring sent transcripts to other contacts and must not loop when OpenClaw's own replies or reactions sync back through Signal.
- Real environment tested: Focused local Vitest coverage and branch autoreview ran in the OpenClaw checkout. Live proof ran against a real linked-device Signal setup using `signal-cli 0.14.3`, an installed local OpenClaw build from this branch, and `channels.signal.ingressMode="note-to-self"` with `receiveMode="on-start"`, `dmPolicy="disabled"`, and `groupPolicy="disabled"`. Agent-driven Signal reaction-action proof also used `tools.profile="messaging"`, because the `coding` profile intentionally removes the `message` tool.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Local verification commands were `pnpm test extensions/signal/src/account-store.test.ts extensions/signal/src/accounts.test.ts extensions/signal/src/approval-auth.test.ts extensions/signal/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts extensions/signal/src/approval-reactions.test.ts extensions/signal/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/signal/src/message-actions.test.ts extensions/signal/src/monitor/event-handler.inbound-context.test.ts extensions/signal/src/monitor.tool-result.autostart.test.ts extensions/signal/src/normalize.test.ts extensions/signal/src/send-reactions.test.ts extensions/signal/src/send.test.ts extensions/signal/src/self-reply-echoes.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`, `pnpm run lint:extensions:bundled`, `pnpm tsgo:prod`, `pnpm check:test-types`, `pnpm run test:extensions:package-boundary:compile`, `pnpm config:channels:check`, `git diff --check`, and `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main`. Packaging and install proof was `pnpm build`, `npm pack`, install the packed local build, `openclaw gateway restart`, `openclaw status --deep`, and `openclaw logs --limit 80 --plain`. Live Signal proof covered these scenarios: phone-originated Note to Self text prompt requesting a unique reply token; phone-originated attachment-only Note to Self image prompt; edited Note to Self prompt changing the requested reply token; `/diagnostics note-to-self approval reaction test` approved by reacting `👍` in Signal; installed local build retested with a fresh Note to Self text prompt; inbound normal DM from another Signal account to the configured Signal number; outbound normal DM from the configured Signal account to another Signal account; direct CLI reaction delivery with `openclaw message react --channel signal --target <redacted-own-signal-account> --message-id <redacted-message-id> --emoji "👀" --json`; agent-driven `message` tool reaction add after `openclaw config set tools.profile messaging` and `openclaw gateway restart`; agent-driven `message` tool reaction add for `✅`; agent-driven `message` tool reaction removal for `✅`.
- Evidence: Unit and integration tests cover Note to Self sent-sync promotion, third-party sent transcript drops, UUID-only paths, edits, attachment-only messages, approval reactions, agent reaction actions, account UUID discovery, authoritative configured signal-cli `dataDir` handling, outbound echo recording, timestamp-less and timestamped media fallback suppression, reaction echo suppression, reaction response-loss ambiguity handling, default-account UUID inheritance/deletion, UUID-preserving allowlists, and legacy approval-reaction key compatibility. Live proof showed Signal channel startup and reply delivery in the gateway log:

```text
channels/signal [default] starting provider (http://127.0.0.1:8080)
signal-cli: INFO HttpServerHandler - Started HTTP server on /127.0.0.1:8080
channels/signal delivered reply to <redacted-own-signal-account>
```

- Observed result: Local verification passed: the focused Signal suite passed with 156 tests, channel config check passed, bundled-extension lint passed, production type checks passed, test type checks passed, package-boundary compile passed, whitespace checks passed, and autoreview reported no accepted/actionable findings. Gateway proof passed: `openclaw status --deep` reported `Signal ON OK configured` on the installed local build. Note to Self text proof passed: a phone-originated Note to Self prompt produced the requested unique token. Note to Self media proof passed: an attachment-only image prompt reached a vision-capable model and the reply described the image text. Note to Self edit proof passed: editing the original Signal message produced a second reply with the edited requested token. Approval reaction proof passed: reacting `👍` to the diagnostics approval prompt resolved the exec approval exactly once with `Exec approval allowed once. Resolved by Signal approval (<redacted-own-signal-account>).` Third-party inbound DM isolation passed: a normal DM from another Signal account arrived on the configured Signal account's phone and linked signal-cli instance, but OpenClaw did not reply, did not send a pairing request, and did not appear to start an agent turn. Third-party outbound sent-sync isolation passed: a normal DM sent from the configured Signal account to another Signal account appeared in signal-cli and on both phones as a regular Signal message, but OpenClaw did not reply or otherwise act on that sent-sync transcript. Direct reaction delivery passed: CLI reaction delivery returned `handledBy: "plugin"`, `ok: true`, and `added: "👀"`. Agent-driven reaction proof passed: with `tools.profile="messaging"`, the agent used the `message` tool to add `👀`, used the `message` tool to add `✅`, then used the `message` tool to remove `✅`. No self-reply loop or reaction-triggered loop was observed in any live proof path.
- What was not tested: Broader Signal device/version coverage is left to CI and maintainer/community testing. Agent-driven reaction actions were live-tested only after enabling the `messaging` tool profile; with `tools.profile="coding"`, the `message` tool is intentionally unavailable and the model replied with text instead of sending a reaction. A daemon-originated self-send was tested and confirmed not to emit a receive event on `/api/v1/events`; the successful Note to Self live proof used the primary phone app, which is the real user path.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: Live Signal proof used one local linked-device Signal setup with redacted account identifiers. It proves the intended personal Note to Self path and the tested isolation cases, not every Signal client/device version.

## Tests and validation

### Which commands did you run?

- `pnpm test extensions/signal/src/account-store.test.ts extensions/signal/src/accounts.test.ts extensions/signal/src/approval-auth.test.ts extensions/signal/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts extensions/signal/src/approval-reactions.test.ts extensions/signal/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/signal/src/message-actions.test.ts extensions/signal/src/monitor/event-handler.inbound-context.test.ts extensions/signal/src/monitor.tool-result.autostart.test.ts extensions/signal/src/normalize.test.ts extensions/signal/src/send-reactions.test.ts extensions/signal/src/send.test.ts extensions/signal/src/self-reply-echoes.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `pnpm run lint:extensions:bundled`
- `pnpm tsgo:prod`
- `pnpm check:test-types`
- `pnpm run test:extensions:package-boundary:compile`
- `pnpm config:channels:check`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main`

### What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Account UUID discovery from signal-cli config/account-store layouts.
- Authoritative configured signal-cli `dataDir` handling, including stale and ambiguous configured stores that fail closed instead of falling back to a different default profile.
- Parse-compatible `accountUuid` handling for shipped string configs, with runtime UUID normalization for bare, compact, and `uuid:<id>` forms.
- Note to Self sync-message promotion for text, nested messages, edits, and attachment-only messages.
- Third-party sent transcript drops for phone and UUID destination mismatches.
- Self-reply echo suppression for timestamp IDs, timestamp-less text/media fallbacks, timestamped media exact/generic fallbacks, provisional pre-send guards, reaction echoes, ambiguous response loss, and late UUID discovery.
- Signal approval reaction matching across phone, compact UUID, hyphenated UUID, and legacy persisted reaction keys.
- Default/named account inheritance behavior for Note to Self mode and `accountUuid`.
- Agent-driven Signal reaction actions, including forwarding resolved account, UUID, and signal-cli config path context into reaction sends.
- UUID-preserving Signal allowlist/security normalization for `uuid:<id>` entries.

### What behavior does this coverage protect?

- Linked-device Note to Self prompts arrive as `syncMessage.sentMessage`, so current behavior needs a self-only path into agent dispatch.
- Note to Self mode needs coverage for UUID identity, attachments, edits, approval reactions, agent reaction actions, third-party transcript drops, and self-reply echoes.

### If no test was added, why not?

- Not applicable; focused regression coverage was added and live behavior was verified.

## Risk checklist

### Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Signal users can opt into a new `note-to-self` ingress mode.

### Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. This adds `channels.signal.ingressMode` and documents optional `channels.signal.accountUuid`, plus generated channel config metadata and docs. Existing configs keep the default `standard` mode; `accountUuid` remains parse-compatible with shipped string configs and only valid UUID forms are used for runtime identity matching.

### Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes, bounded to Signal channel ingress and native approval reaction handling. There are no new external network calls or provider/tool permissions.

### What is the highest-risk area?

- Accidentally treating sent transcripts to other Signal recipients as user prompts, or letting OpenClaw's own Note to Self replies loop back into the agent.

### How is that risk mitigated?

- The mode is explicit opt-in and Signal-local.
- Self-addressed sent-sync promotion checks source/destination identity, including UUID-only envelopes and valid runtime UUID normalization.
- Sent transcripts to other recipients are dropped before dispatch.
- Self-reply echo suppression records outbound replies, media fallbacks, and reaction fingerprints in process memory under the effective Signal phone/UUID identities.
- Focused tests cover destination mismatch drops, UUID normalization, edits, attachments, approval reactions, agent reaction actions, and echo suppression.

## Current review state

### What is the next action?

- Ready for maintainer review.

### What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- Nothing known from the author side. CI and maintainer review are still needed after PR creation.

### Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Local branch autoreview reports no accepted/actionable findings. ClawSweeper's `accountUuid` compatibility review is addressed by permissive schema parsing with runtime UUID normalization.

## Notes For Maintainers

- This intentionally does not edit `CHANGELOG.md`; release-owned changelog generation should pick up the PR/commit context.
- `channels.signal.accountUuid` intentionally remains parse-compatible with shipped string configs. Runtime identity matching only treats valid bare, compact, or legacy `uuid:<id>` UUID forms as UUIDs; docs recommend bare UUIDs and normal users should rely on auto-discovery instead.
- If a maintainer wants live Signal proof before merge, the code is structured so the requested scenario can be demonstrated without changing the PR: configure `ingressMode: "note-to-self"`, start the Signal monitor, and send a Note to Self message from the primary device.

target.title: fix: skip setup-only channel plugins in outbound resolution and drain pending deliveries on qqbot reconnect
target.body:
## Summary

When the qqbot WebSocket reconnects after a session timeout (close code `4009`), `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` returns a setup-only plugin stub that lacks any send capability. This causes the `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` error, and all messages queued during the disconnection window are permanently lost.

## Root Cause

`resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` resolves the first matching plugin entry for a channel id without verifying that the entry actually has send capability (`outbound.sendText`, `message.send.text`, etc.). During qqbot WebSocket reconnection, a setup-only plugin entry (registered for configuration/pairing but not for message delivery) shadows the real sending plugin, causing the resolution to short-circuit with a non-functional stub.

Regression introduced in 2026.5.28 (commit `e932160`).

## Fix

### 1. Split outbound channel resolution into shared and delivery paths (`channel-resolution.ts`)

Add a `channelPluginCanSend()` predicate that checks for any of the 10 known send methods across both the legacy `outbound` adapter and the newer `message.send` adapter:

- `outbound.sendText / sendMedia / sendPayload / sendPoll / sendFormattedText / sendFormattedMedia`
- `message.send.text / media / payload / poll`

Instead of gating the shared `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` resolver (which would break action-only channel plugins that have `actions.handleAction` but no send capability), a new **`resolveOutboundChannelPluginForDelivery()`** resolver applies the send-capability guard exclusively for message delivery paths.

- **`resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()`**: Shared resolver — returns any matching plugin regardless of send capability, preserving resolution for action dispatch, target resolution, and other non-send callers.
- **`resolveOutboundChannelPluginForDelivery()`**: Delivery resolver — skips setup-only entries and only returns plugins that can actually send messages.
- **`resolveOutboundChannelMessageAdapter()`**: Updated to use the delivery resolver.
- **`message.ts`**: `sendMessage`/`sendPoll` updated to use the delivery resolver.

### 2. Drain pending deliveries on qqbot reconnect (`extensions/qqbot/src/channel.ts`)

The `onReady` and `onResumed` gateway callbacks now call `drainPendingDeliveries()` from the plugin SDK delivery queue runtime, recovering messages that were queued while the WebSocket was disconnected. The drain is:

- Filtered by channel (`qqbot`) and account id, with a fallback for default-account entries where `accountId` may be `undefined`
- Deduplicated via `drainKey` to prevent concurrent drains
- Wrapped in try/catch so drain failures do not break the reconnection flow

## Tests

- `channel-resolution.test.ts`: 17 tests (5 new — setup-only plugin returns from shared resolver, action-only plugin resolves from shared but not delivery resolver, delivery resolver skips setup-only and returns sending fallback, legacy outbound adapter, poll-only outbound)
- `channel.test.ts`: 2 tests (new file — onReady drain, onResumed drain)

Fixes #88955.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: After a QQ Bot WebSocket disconnect (session takeover / close code 4009), `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin()` returned a setup-only plugin stub instead of the runtime entry, causing `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` and dropping all pending deliveries.

- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw `2026.6.2` (commit `b90683e`, branch `fix/qqbot-reconnect-outbound-88955`), macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0. QQ Bot plugin `@openclaw/qqbot@2026.5.31-beta.4` with live QQ Bot credentials (appId `<redacted>`).

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Built patched OpenClaw from fix branch: `pnpm build && npm pack --ignore-scripts`
  2. Installed globally: `npm install -g openclaw-2026.6.2.tgz` — confirmed version: `OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (b90683e)`
  3. Restarted gateway: `openclaw gateway restart`
  4. Observed qqbot plugin startup, WebSocket connection, and `Gateway ready` callback sequence in logs
  5. Verified normal QQ Bot C2C message round-trip: user sent "hello" → bot received → processed → replied with markdown (20 chars, 200 OK)
  6. Scanned full gateway log for `"Outbound not configured"` errors — zero matches
  7. Repeated restart cycle to confirm reconnect stability across multiple `Gateway ready` events

- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  # Patched OpenClaw installed and running
  $ openclaw --version
  OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (b90683e)

  # Gateway restart sequence — qqbot reconnects cleanly
  2026-06-02T22:38:53.727+08:00 [gateway] received SIGTERM; restarting
  2026-06-02T22:39:15.986+08:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: ..., qqbot, ...; 1.6s)
  2026-06-02T22:39:16.171+08:00 [qqbot] [qqbot:default] Starting gateway — appId=<redacted>, enabled=true
  2026-06-02T22:39:17.387+08:00 [qqbot] [default] Connecting to wss://api.sgroup.qq.com/websocket
  2026-06-02T22:39:18.468+08:00 [qqbot] [default] WebSocket connected
  2026-06-02T22:39:18.641+08:00 [qqbot] [qqbot:default] Gateway ready

  # Successful QQ Bot message delivery after reconnect
  2026-06-02T22:32:40.229+08:00 [qqbot] [default] Processing message from <redacted>: hello
  2026-06-02T22:32:59.431+08:00 [qqbot] [default] [qqbot:api] <<< Status: 200 OK
  2026-06-02T22:32:59.432+08:00 [qqbot] [default] Sent markdown chunk (20/20 chars) with 0 HTTP images (c2c)

  # Zero outbound resolution errors across entire log history
  $ grep -c "Outbound not configured.*qqbot" ~/Library/Logs/openclaw/gateway.log
  0

  # Multiple Gateway ready events over 4+ hours — all clean reconnects
  $ grep "qqbot.*Gateway ready" ~/Library/Logs/openclaw/gateway.log | wc -l
  18
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: The delivery resolver correctly skipped the setup-only plugin entry (which has no `sendText`/`sendMedia` methods) and resolved the runtime entry with full send capability. Outbound messages delivered successfully through the QQ Bot API (HTTP 200). Zero occurrences of the `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` error across 18 reconnect cycles over a 4+ hour observation window. The shared resolver continued to return all plugin entries for non-send callers (action dispatch, target resolution), preserving action-only plugin compatibility.

- **What was not tested**:

  Live drain proof is not reproducible on the fixed build. The `drainReconnectPendingDeliveries()` mechanism (Fix 2) was verified via unit tests but not observed draining actual queued entries in a live environment. This is not an oversight — it is a structural consequence of the resolver fix (Fix 1) eliminating the only deterministic cause of pending qqbot entries. Code-level analysis confirms this:

  **How the delivery queue works** (`deliver.ts:1254-1280`): Every outbound delivery is write-ahead enqueued via `enqueueDelivery()` before any send attempt. On success, `ackDelivery()` removes the entry (`deliver.ts:1367`). On throw, `failDelivery()` bumps `retryCount` and **leaves it pending** (`delivery-queue-storage.ts:137-144`).

  **What created the pending entries in #88955**: The `"Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"` throw fired from `createChannelHandler` (`deliver.ts:223-231`) when `resolveOutboundChannelPluginForDelivery` returned a setup-only plugin with no send methods. This throw occurs **outside** the per-payload try/catch (`deliver.ts:1451`), so it propagated to the queue-cleanup catch → `failDelivery` → entry left pending. The resolver fix eliminates this throw by filtering setup-only entries via `channelPluginCanSend()`.

  **Why transient QQ API failures also do not create pending entries**: The qqbot engine **swallows** HTTP/network errors into returned `{error}` result objects instead of throwing (`outbound.ts:301-304, 313-318`). The delivery core text branch records "sent" purely on `deliveredResults.length > 0` with no message-id identity gate (`deliver.ts:1772-1778`), so a swallowed engine error yields a "sent" outcome → `ackDelivery` → entry removed, not pending.

  **The drain's real value**: It is a belt-and-suspenders safeguard for the **upgrade path** — entries left pending (with `lastError: "Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"`) by an unfixed build are drained immediately after a fixed gateway reconnects. The `bypassBackoff` predicate explicitly matches this exact error string (`/outbound not configured/i`), ensuring these entries skip retry backoff and are retried on the first reconnect event. If the retry still fails (e.g., the plugin is genuinely misconfigured), `isPermanentDeliveryError` (`delivery-queue-recovery.ts:79`) moves the entry to `failed/` — no infinite retry loop. `MAX_RETRIES` is also enforced before the bypass branch (`delivery-queue-recovery.ts:544`). The drain also covers the narrow crash-in-enqueue-to-ack window handled by `recoverPendingDeliveries` at startup (`delivery-queue-recovery.ts:604`). The drain selection filter (`channel === "qqbot"` + account id match + bypassBackoff for `"Outbound not configured"`, `channel.test.ts:90-152`) and the reconnect-drain integration logic (`delivery-queue.reconnect-drain.test.ts`) are exercised by 35 unit tests (all passing).

  **Seeded pending-drain proof**: To validate the drain recovery path end-to-end, a pending delivery entry was manually seeded into the SQLite delivery queue to simulate the upgrade scenario (entries left by an unfixed build):

  1. Inserted a pending entry: `id=seeded-drain-test-001`, `channel=qqbot`, `accountId=default`, `lastError="Outbound not configured for channel: qqbot"`, `retry_count=1`
  2. Restarted gateway: `openclaw gateway restart`
  3. Observed recovery sequence in logs:

  ```
  00:30:00.353 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: ..., qqbot, ...; 1.9s)
  00:30:00.987 [delivery-recovery] Found 1 pending delivery entries — starting recovery
  00:30:01.567 [qqbot] [qqbot:api] <<< Status: 200 OK
  00:30:02.703 [delivery-recovery] Recovered delivery seeded-drain-test-001 on qqbot
  00:30:02.703 [delivery-recovery] Delivery recovery complete: 1 recovered, 0 failed, 0 skipped (max retries), 0 deferred (backoff)
  00:30:02.816 [qqbot] [default] WebSocket connected
  00:30:02.952 [qqbot] [qqbot:default] Gateway ready
  ```

  4. Verified entry removed from DB: `SELECT count(*) FROM delivery_queue_entries WHERE channel='qqbot' AND status='pending'` → **0**
  5. Zero `"Outbound not configured"` errors after restart

  **Key observation**: The `0 deferred (backoff)` in the recovery summary confirms the `bypassBackoff` predicate (`/outbound not configured/i`) correctly matched the seeded entry's `lastError`, allowing immediate retry without waiting for backoff. The entry was successfully delivered (HTTP 200) and acked (removed from queue).

  **Note**: The startup `recoverPendingDeliveries` path (`delivery-queue-recovery.ts:604`) executed before the qqbot `onReady` callback (`00:30:00.987` vs `00:30:02.952`), so startup recovery handled the entry before the reconnect drain fired. Both paths share the same underlying drain logic and bypass predicates; the startup path simply runs first. A naturally-occurring live drain on the `onReady` path cannot be reliably reproduced because the fix removes the deterministic failure that created those entries, and startup recovery pre-empts the reconnect drain for entries present at boot. Multi-shard configurations were not tested.

target.title: fix(cron): skip deleteAfterRun and preserve counters for manual runs (#83538)
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw cron run` (manual/debug runs) on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes the job on success — consuming the one-shot and preventing its scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters, interfering with scheduled retry/backoff state.

This PR adds an `isManual` flag to `applyJobResult` so manual runs preserve the job and leave scheduled execution state untouched.

## Root cause

`applyJobResult()` has no concept of manual vs scheduled runs. When a manual run succeeds on a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job, `shouldDelete` evaluates to `true` and the job is removed from the store — the scheduled fire never happens.

Additionally, manual error/skipped/success outcomes unconditionally mutate `consecutiveErrors` and `consecutiveSkipped` counters, which control retry backoff and auto-disable behavior for scheduled runs.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `timer.ts` | Add `isManual` option to `applyJobResult`; skip `deleteAfterRun` for manual runs; guard all counter mutations and alert emissions with `!isManual` |
| `ops.ts` | Pass `isManual: true` from manual run completion and invalid-persisted-run handling |
| `timer.regression.test.ts` | 5 new tests: manual ok/error preserve job, manual doesn't increment/reset counters |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts
 ✓ cron (45 tests) 144ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  45 passed (45)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes it, preventing scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate scheduled retry counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `aa92a4a5b0`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.

### Source-level proof

```
Main branch applyJobResult():
  - shouldDelete = kind === "at" && deleteAfterRun && status === "ok"
    → no manual discriminator, always deletes on success
  - consecutiveErrors incremented unconditionally on error
  - consecutiveSkipped incremented unconditionally on skip

Fix branch:
  - shouldDelete = wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual
    → manual runs never trigger deletion
  - Counter mutations guarded: if (!opts?.isManual) { ... }
    → manual runs don't affect retry/backoff state
```

### Regression test coverage

```
✅ manual ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted
✅ manual error on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted  
✅ scheduled ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job deleted (unchanged behavior)
✅ manual error does not increment consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
✅ manual success does not reset consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
```

- **What was not tested**: Live `openclaw cron run` CLI flow (requires a running gateway with a configured `deleteAfterRun` at-job). The `isManual` flag propagation from `ops.ts` to `applyJobResult` is validated at the unit level.

Closes #83538


### Re-verification (2026-05-20)

Local checkout at `d89f14de26`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

 ✓ cron  ../../src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (39 tests) 116ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  39 passed (39)
   Duration  152ms
```

Full cron suite (`src/cron/`) also run: 94 test files, 901 passed. The 7 failures are in unrelated files (`run-log.test.ts`, `service.failure-alert.test.ts`) pre-existing on upstream/main.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: `openclaw cron run` (manual) of a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job incorrectly deletes the job. Manual runs also pollute `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched checkout at `0cec2acc7a`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On `upstream/main`, counter mutations and `shouldDelete` run unconditionally — no `isManual` discriminator exists.
- **Exact steps after patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts` + `src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: 46/46 timer regression tests pass (including 7 new #83538/#83933-specific tests). 7/7 failure-alert tests pass.
- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI flow; the tests exercise the same `applyJobResult` code path the Gateway calls internally.

### Key test results

| Scenario | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Manual run + deleteAfterRun + success | NOT deleted ✅ |
| Scheduled run + deleteAfterRun + success | Deleted ✅ |
| Manual error + consecutiveErrors=3 | Stays 3 ✅ |
| Manual success + consecutiveErrors=5 | Stays 5 ✅ |
| Manual error → failure alert | NOT triggered ✅ |
| Scheduled error → failure alert | Triggered ✅ |


## Real behavior proof (Docker, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker container, `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched source at `885d605e56`.

### Latest fixes

| Issue | Problem | Fix |
|-------|---------|-----|
| P1 | Manual at-job error was disabling job (`enabled=false`, `nextRunAtMs=undefined`) | At-job error retry/disable block now guarded with `if (opts?.isManual)` |
| P2 | Manual success was clearing `lastFailureAlertAtMs` cooldown | Cooldown clearing now inside `if (!opts?.isManual)` guard |

### Test results

| Test Suite | Result |
|------------|--------|
| Timer regression (49 tests) | ✅ All passed |
| Failure-alert (7 tests) | ✅ All passed |

### Key verified behaviors

| Scenario | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Manual at-job error | `enabled` stays true, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅ |
| Manual success | `lastFailureAlertAtMs` cooldown preserved ✅ |
| Manual run + deleteAfterRun | Job NOT deleted ✅ |
| Scheduled run + deleteAfterRun | Job deleted ✅ |
| Manual error + consecutiveErrors=3 | Stays 3 ✅ |
| Manual success + consecutiveErrors=5 | Stays 5 ✅ |

| A+B: counters | `cron runs` | `consecutiveErrors` mutated | Unchanged ✅ |

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` no longer consumes `deleteAfterRun` one-shot jobs, and no longer perturbs retry counters, at-job retry/disable state, recurring backoff, or failure-alert cooldown.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container built from PR HEAD `3d32c9baff`, Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.14.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `applyJobResult` has no manual-run discriminator — manual runs unconditionally drive `deleteAfterRun`, counter mutations, at-job disable, recurring backoff, and alert cooldown. The 12 regression tests added by this PR do not exist on main.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Build Docker image from PR HEAD
docker build --target build -t openclaw-pr83933:test .

# Test 1: Timer regression tests (includes all 12 manual-run isolation tests)

# Test 4: Full cron test suite
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/
```

The 2 failures are in `cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts` — these look for Swift source files (`apps/macos/Sources/OpenClaw/CronModels.swift`) which are not present in the Docker build target. These failures are pre-existing and unrelated to this PR.

- **Observed result after fix**: All 12 new regression tests pass, confirming that manual runs preserve one-shot jobs (`deleteAfterRun`), do not increment/reset counters, do not disable at-jobs on error, do not rewrite recurring `nextRunAtMs` with backoff, and do not clear failure-alert cooldown. Control-group tests confirm scheduled runs still drive all 5 state effects normally.

- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI `openclaw cron run` end-to-end (covered by earlier Docker CLI proof at `642c3450d9` — subsequent commits were formatting-only changes via oxfmt). The `cron-protocol-conformance` tests require macOS app source and cannot run in Docker.


## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `6bc3b9e374`, 2026-05-30)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` no longer consumes `deleteAfterRun` one-shot jobs, and no longer perturbs retry counters, at-job retry/disable state, recurring backoff, failure-alert cooldown, or skipped-run alert counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container built from PR HEAD `6bc3b9e374`, Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.14.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `applyJobResult` has no manual-run discriminator — manual runs unconditionally drive all 5 scheduled-state effects plus skipped-run alert paths. The 14 regression tests added by this PR do not exist on main.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Build Docker image from PR HEAD
docker build --target build -t openclaw-pr83933:test .

# Test 1: Timer regression tests (all 14 manual-run isolation tests)
```

The 2 failures are in `cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts` — these look for Swift source files (`apps/macos/Sources/OpenClaw/CronModels.swift`) which are not present in the Docker build target. Pre-existing and unrelated to this PR.

- **Observed result after fix**: All 14 new regression tests pass, covering all 6 guarded state effects including the newly-added manual skipped-run isolation:
  - Manual `deleteAfterRun` at-job → NOT deleted ✅
  - Manual error → counters NOT incremented ✅
  - Manual success → counters NOT reset, cooldown NOT cleared ✅
  - Manual at-job error → NOT disabled, nextRunAtMs preserved ✅
  - Manual every-job error → backoff NOT applied, nextRunAtMs preserved ✅
  - Manual skipped at-job → consecutiveSkipped NOT incremented, alert NOT fired, all state preserved ✅
  - Manual skipped run with `includeSkipped: true` → failure alert NOT emitted ✅

  Control-group tests confirm scheduled runs still drive all state effects normally.

- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI `openclaw cron run` end-to-end (covered by earlier Docker CLI proof at `642c3450d9` — subsequent commits were formatting + test coverage changes). The `cron-protocol-conformance` tests require macOS app source and cannot run in Docker.

### Manual-run state contract

`cron run` is a debug/verification command. Manual runs record their outcome (lastRunAtMs, lastStatus, lastError) but do not advance scheduled state. This makes manual runs safe, non-destructive operations that cannot interfere with scheduled execution. No reasonable user scenario depends on manual runs consuming one-shot jobs or driving retry/backoff state.

## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `ccb89e3f11`, 2026-06-02)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `lastRunWasManual` field was missing from the `CronJobStateSchema` and `CronJobStatePatchSchema` in `packages/gateway-protocol/src/schema/cron.ts`. Both schemas use `additionalProperties: false`, so returning a cron job state containing `lastRunWasManual` would fail gateway protocol validation on `cron list`/`cron show`. Added `lastRunWasManual: Type.Optional(Type.Boolean())` to both schemas.

- **Real environment tested**: Docker container (`node:24-bookworm`), Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.16.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`, patched OpenClaw at commit `ccb89e3f11`.

- **Failing proof before fix**: On the previous commit (`45efd1abdd`), `CronJobStateSchema` did not include `lastRunWasManual`. Any cron job state object with `lastRunWasManual: true` (set by `timer.ts:414` after a manual run) would be rejected by TypeBox validation due to `additionalProperties: false`. The `CronJobStatePatchSchema` was similarly missing the field, preventing state patches that include it.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
docker run --rm \
  -v "$(pwd):/app" \
  -v /app/node_modules \
  -e NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=8192" \
  node:24-bookworm \
  bash -c '
    cd /app && npm install 2>&1 | tail -3 && \
    node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
      src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts \
      src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts \
      src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts \
      src/cron/cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts \
      src/cron/cron-protocol-schema.test.ts 2>&1'
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```

 ✓ cron src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts (15 tests) 54ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts (10 tests) 31ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts (3 tests) 3ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/cron-protocol-schema.test.ts (2 tests) 0ms

 Test Files  5 passed (5)
      Tests  86 passed (86)
   Start at  07:37:28
   Duration  3.44s (transform 2.31s, setup 280ms, import 2.62s, tests 424ms, environment 0ms)
```

- **Observed result after fix**: All 86 tests pass including:
  - `timer.regression.test.ts`: 56 tests — manual run sets `lastRunWasManual: true` on state (line 2531), state now passes schema validation.
  - `service.restart-catchup.test.ts`: 15 tests — restart catch-up checks `lastRunWasManual` in state fixture (line 224) and correctly re-runs the job.
  - `cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts`: 3 tests — schema conformance verified (delivery modes, status shape, failover reasons).
  - `cron-protocol-schema.test.ts`: 2 tests — schema structure validated (deprecated alias, failure notification state).

- **What was not tested**: Live `cron list`/`cron show` gateway roundtrip with the patched schema. The schema fix is structural (adding a field to a TypeBox object with `additionalProperties: false`) and the test suite validates that state objects with `lastRunWasManual` are accepted by the schema.

## Live Gateway CLI roundtrip proof (Docker, HEAD `ccb89e3f11`)

The `lastRunWasManual` field must pass `CronJobStateSchema` validation (which uses `additionalProperties: false`).
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container `node:24-bookworm` (linux/arm64, OrbStack), Node.js `v24.16.0`, OpenClaw Gateway built from source at commit `ccb89e3f11`.

  - `deleteAfterRun: true` — still set, NOT consumed by manual run ✅
  - `lastRunWasManual: true` — new field present and accepted by schema ✅
  - No `consecutiveErrors` field — manual error did NOT increment counter ✅
  - Run history shows `runId: "manual:..."` prefix confirming manual execution path ✅
  - Both `cron show` and `cron list` returned `lastRunWasManual` without schema validation errors, proving `CronJobStateSchema` correctly includes the field ✅

- **What was not tested**: Manual run with `status: "ok"` (would require a working LLM provider in Docker). The `shouldDelete` guard path (`wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual`) is exercised by unit tests. Multi-node cluster, production load.


## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `8596607fca`, 2026-06-02 — backoff fix)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a recurring job that errors must not open a backoff window that blocks the next scheduled fire. Previously, `isErrorBackoffPending()`, `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()`, `isPendingErrorBackoffSlot()`, and `recomputeJobNextRunAtMs()` read `lastRunStatus` and `lastRunAtMs` without checking `lastRunWasManual`, causing a manual error to push `nextRunAtMs` forward and block the timer-scheduled fire.

- **Real environment tested**: Docker container `node:24-bookworm` (linux/arm64), Node.js `v24.16.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`, patched OpenClaw at commit `8596607fca`.

- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` does not check `lastRunWasManual`. A recurring every-job with `lastRunWasManual: true` and `lastRunStatus: "error"` would have `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` return a backoff deadline, `isErrorBackoffPending()` would return `true`, and `isRunnableJob()` would return `false` — blocking the next scheduled fire. `recomputeJobNextRunAtMs()` would also push `nextRunAtMs` forward to the backoff floor. Test file authored as part of this PR.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Unit tests in Docker
docker run --rm --platform linux/arm64 \
  -v "$(pwd):/app" -w /app \
  -e NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=8192" -e CI=true \
  node:24-bookworm bash -c \
  "node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts \
    src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts \
    src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts"

# Live Gateway CLI in Docker
docker run --rm --platform linux/arm64 \
  -v "$(pwd):/app" -w /app \
  -e NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=8192" -e CI=true \
  -e OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-test \
  -e OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN=test-token-123 \
  node:24-bookworm bash -c '
    mkdir -p /tmp/openclaw-test
    echo "{\"gateway\":{\"port\":19001,\"bind\":\"loopback\"}}" > /tmp/openclaw-test/openclaw.json
    node openclaw.mjs gateway run --dev --allow-unconfigured --token test-token-123 --port 19001 &
    sleep 8
    node openclaw.mjs cron add --name test-at-delete --at "2026-06-02T23:00:00Z" --message reminder --delete-after-run --url ws://127.0.0.1:19001 --token test-token-123
    # extract ID, then: cron run <ID>, cron show <ID>, cron list
    node openclaw.mjs cron add --name test-recurring --every 10s --message "recurring check" --url ws://127.0.0.1:19001 --token test-token-123
    # extract ID, then: cron run <ID>, cron show <ID>
    kill %1'
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

<details>
<summary>Docker unit test output (82 tests pass)</summary>

```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (57 tests) 254ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts (15 tests) 44ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts (10 tests) 25ms

 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  82 passed (82)
   Start at  11:08:37
   Duration  2.77s (transform 1.83s, setup 250ms, import 2.09s, tests 323ms, environment 0ms)

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 5.85s
```

</details>

<details>
<summary>Docker Gateway CLI output</summary>

```
=== Node.js version ===
v24.16.0
=== Starting Gateway ===
[gateway] ready

TEST 1: Create deleteAfterRun at-job
  → id: dbfbe16b-c44a-4aa1-b820-889dbdbcb9a2, deleteAfterRun: true, nextRunAtMs: 1780441200000

TEST 2: Manual run on deleteAfterRun at-job
  → { "ok": true, "enqueued": true, "runId": "manual:dbfbe16b-..." }

TEST 3: Verify at-job survived manual run
  → id: dbfbe16b-..., enabled: true, deleteAfterRun: true, nextRunAtMs: 1780441200000 (PRESERVED)
  → status: "running" (agent executing but will error — no LLM provider in Docker)

TEST 4: Create recurring every-10s job
  → id: 18b2583a-7990-4667-95cd-591614c858f9, everyMs: 10000, nextRunAtMs: 1780398505454

TEST 5: Manual run on recurring job
  → { "ok": true, "enqueued": true, "runId": "manual:18b2583a-..." }

TEST 6: Verify recurring job state after manual run
  → id: 18b2583a-..., enabled: true, nextRunAtMs: 1780398505454 (PRESERVED, NOT pushed by backoff)

TEST 7: Schema validation — cron list --json
  → Both jobs returned with valid schema, no validation errors
  → total: 2, both enabled, both with preserved nextRunAtMs

Gateway stopped.
```

</details>

- **Observed result after fix**:
  1. **deleteAfterRun at-job manual run**: Job survived manual run, `enabled: true`, `deleteAfterRun: true`, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅
  2. **Recurring job manual run**: Job's `nextRunAtMs` preserved at natural cadence, NOT pushed forward by backoff ✅
  3. **Schema validation**: Both `cron list --json` and `cron show --json` returned `lastRunWasManual` field without validation errors ✅
  4. **New regression test**: `#83933: manual error on recurring job does not block scheduled fire via backoff` — verifies `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` returns `undefined` for manual errors, `isRunnableJob()` returns `true` within theoretical backoff window, `recomputeNextRunsForMaintenance()` computes natural cadence (not backoff floor), and control case confirms scheduled errors still apply backoff ✅

- **What was not tested**: Manual run with `status: "ok"` on recurring job (requires working LLM in Docker). Multi-node cluster, production load, actual delivery to chat channels.

The PR changes Amazon Bedrock memory credential detection so unconfigured no-signal checks skip the AWS SDK/IMDS path while explicit Bedrock memory configuration still allows the default AWS credential chain, with focused tests.

PR surface: Source +134, Tests +128. Total +262 across 4 files.

Reproducibility: yes. at source level. Current main can fall through from Bedrock memory credential detection into the AWS SDK IMDS provider, and the linked issue includes sandbox logs matching the SDK's PUT token and GET credentials paths.

target.title: fix(amazon-bedrock): skip IMDS during credential auto-detection (#64891)
target.body:
﻿## Summary

- Stop Bedrock memory auto-detection from probing AWS IMDS (`169.254.169.254`) when no AWS credential signals are configured.
- Keep the AWS SDK default credential chain available for explicitly selected/configured Bedrock memory providers, including shared config/profile, SSO, web identity, ECS, and EC2/IMDS role setups.
- During non-IMDS profile/container/web-identity probes, temporarily set `AWS_EC2_METADATA_DISABLED=true` so `defaultProvider()` skips instance metadata while still resolving configured non-IMDS sources.
- Memoize `process.env` credential probes for 5 minutes and coalesce concurrent calls without sharing cached results across IMDS-enabled and IMDS-disabled probe modes.

Closes #64891

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Bedrock memory auto-detection no longer loads the AWS SDK credential chain, and therefore does not reach IMDS, when there are no AWS credential signals. Explicit Bedrock memory configuration still allows the full AWS SDK default chain.

Real environment tested: Windows local OpenClaw source checkout, running the patched Amazon Bedrock provider code directly through Node 22/tsx with AWS credential environment variables cleared.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```powershell
node --import tsx -e "const awsKeys=['AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID','AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY','AWS_SESSION_TOKEN','AWS_PROFILE','AWS_BEARER_TOKEN_BEDROCK','AWS_CONTAINER_CREDENTIALS_RELATIVE_URI','AWS_CONTAINER_CREDENTIALS_FULL_URI','AWS_WEB_IDENTITY_TOKEN_FILE','AWS_ROLE_ARN','AWS_EC2_METADATA_DISABLED']; for (const key of awsKeys) delete process.env[key]; const { hasAwsCredentials } = await import('./extensions/amazon-bedrock/embedding-provider.ts'); const started=Date.now(); const result=await hasAwsCredentials(); console.log(JSON.stringify({setup:'local OpenClaw source checkout',command:'hasAwsCredentials() with AWS credential env cleared',awsCredentialEnvPresent:awsKeys.filter((key)=>process.env[key]),result,elapsedMs:Date.now()-started,metadataDisabledAfter:process.env.AWS_EC2_METADATA_DISABLED ?? null},null,2));"
```

Evidence after fix: Console output from the local OpenClaw source checkout:

```json
{
  "setup": "local OpenClaw source checkout",
  "command": "hasAwsCredentials() with AWS credential env cleared",
  "awsCredentialEnvPresent": [],
  "result": false,
  "elapsedMs": 0,
  "metadataDisabledAfter": null
}
```

Observed result after fix: With AWS credential environment cleared, the real provider code returned `false` immediately from the no-signal auto-detection path. The command completed without setting `AWS_EC2_METADATA_DISABLED` and without waiting on the AWS SDK metadata timeout path.

What was not tested: A live AWS EC2/ECS/default-chain credential setup and the original non-AWS sandbox packet trace were not available in this environment; those remain maintainer/live-environment proof gaps.

## Tests

- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/amazon-bedrock/embedding-provider.test.ts extensions/amazon-bedrock/memory-embedding-adapter.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-providers.config.ts`
- `pnpm exec tsgo -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --pretty false`
- `pnpm exec tsgo -p tsconfig.extensions.json --pretty false`
- `OPENCLAW_EXTENSION_BOUNDARY_CONCURRENCY=1 pnpm run test:extensions:package-boundary:compile`
- `OPENCLAW_EXTENSION_BOUNDARY_CONCURRENCY=1 pnpm run test:extensions:package-boundary:canary`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/amazon-bedrock/embedding-provider.ts extensions/amazon-bedrock/embedding-provider.test.ts extensions/amazon-bedrock/memory-embedding-adapter.ts extensions/amazon-bedrock/memory-embedding-adapter.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Test plan

- [x] Empty env skips credential SDK load
- [x] `AWS_EC2_METADATA_DISABLED=true` exits without SDK when no probe signals
- [x] `AWS_PROFILE` probe disables IMDS temporarily and restores env
- [x] Explicit Bedrock provider/config preserves full AWS SDK default chain probing
- [x] `process.env` probes are memoized and coalesced per IMDS mode
- [ ] Maintainer/live env: non-AWS sandbox startup shows no repeated `169.254.169.254` traffic
- [ ] Maintainer/live env: EC2/ECS/default-chain Bedrock memory credentials still resolve

target.title: fix(heartbeat): suppress no-op system event replies
target.body:
## Summary
- add centralized no-op sentinel detection for silent heartbeat/system-event replies
- suppress exact `HEARTBEAT_OK`, `NO_REPLY`, `NO_NEW_AUDIO`, and `SESSION_WATCHDOG_OK` outputs before delivery
- keep meaningful relayable exec summaries deliverable without moving cron events out of heartbeat-mode ack suppression
- preserve newer heartbeat prefix/model-selection behavior after rebasing onto current `origin/main`

Fixes #73149.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Heartbeat/system-event handoffs could visibly deliver no-op sentinel replies such as exact `HEARTBEAT_OK`, while still needing to deliver meaningful exec/system-event summaries.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout for PR #73785 on Linux/Node 22 after rebasing onto current `origin/main` at `3de97057d0`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `node --import tsx tmp/pr73785-runtime-proof.mjs` from the patched checkout. The script imported the patched `runHeartbeatOnce`, used the real in-process system-event queue, registered the channel plugin send path with outbound capture, seeded a real file-backed session store, and exercised both cron-event and exec-event paths.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output copied from the after-fix proof command:

  ```json
  {
    "cron": {
      "name": "cron",
      "result": {
        "status": "ran",
        "durationMs": 27
      },
      "provider": "cron-event",
      "sent": []
    },
    "exec": {
      "name": "exec",
      "result": {
        "status": "ran",
        "durationMs": 1257
      },
      "provider": "exec-event",
      "sent": [
        {
          "to": "120363140186826074@g.us",
          "text": "Command completed: uploaded report.txt"
        }
      ]
    }
  }
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** A cron-event reply of `HEARTBEAT_OK Watchdog clean` stayed in heartbeat-mode short-ack suppression and produced no outbound send. A meaningful exec-event reply still crossed the send boundary as `Command completed: uploaded report.txt`.
- **What was not tested:** No real WhatsApp or Telegram message was sent; the proof used a dummy WhatsApp group id and captured the outbound send locally to avoid test noise in real channels.

## Validation
- `CI=true pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/agents/model-runtime-policy.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.ts src/auto-reply/tokens.ts src/infra/heartbeat-runner.ts src/infra/heartbeat-runner.respects-ackmaxchars-heartbeat-acks.test.ts`
- `CI=true pnpm exec oxlint src/agents/model-runtime-policy.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.ts src/auto-reply/tokens.ts src/infra/heartbeat-runner.ts src/infra/heartbeat-runner.respects-ackmaxchars-heartbeat-acks.test.ts`
- `CI=true pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.infra.config.ts src/infra/heartbeat-runner.respects-ackmaxchars-heartbeat-acks.test.ts` — 16/16 passed
- `CI=true pnpm check:test-types`

target.title: fix(gateway): bind MCP loopback scope to tokens
target.body:
## Summary
- Bind MCP loopback session/account/channel context to server-issued scoped bearer tokens.
- Stop emitting mutable loopback scope headers in bundled MCP config and CLI env.
- Add regressions proving spoofed scope/owner headers do not change loopback tool context.

## Motivation
The previous April owner-context fix made `senderIsOwner` derive from owner/non-owner bearer tokens, but the same loopback endpoint still accepted `x-session-key`, `x-openclaw-account-id`, `x-openclaw-message-channel`, and `x-openclaw-inbound-event-kind` from every request. A process with a valid loopback bearer could therefore keep non-owner status but choose a different session/account/channel/event policy context. Those fields feed `resolveGatewayScopedTools(...)`, group/session policy, room-event message-tool behavior, and hook context.

This PR closes that adjacent trust-boundary gap by issuing per-run scoped loopback tokens and resolving the full MCP loopback context from the server-side token registry instead of request headers.

## Rebase notes (current revision)
Rebased onto current `main`, which had grown five more header-driven context fields on the same loopback surface (`x-openclaw-current-channel-id`, `x-openclaw-current-thread-ts`, `x-openclaw-current-message-id`, `x-openclaw-current-inbound-audio`, `x-openclaw-source-reply-delivery-mode`). Those are the same spoofable surface, so the scoped token now carries the full context: `sessionKey`, `messageProvider`, `currentChannelId`, `currentThreadTs`, `currentMessageId`, `currentInboundAudio`, `accountId`, `inboundEventKind`, `sourceReplyDeliveryMode`, `senderIsOwner`. The bundled MCP server config now sends only the `Authorization` bearer header; all `x-*` scope headers and their `OPENCLAW_MCP_*` env plumbing are gone. The live-probe helper keeps main's request timeout + bounded body read and issues/revokes a scoped owner token per call.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: MCP loopback requests with spoofed scope and owner headers should still resolve tool context from the server-issued scoped bearer token.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, running the actual `src/gateway/mcp-http.ts` loopback server with `OPENCLAW_CLI_BACKEND_LOG_OUTPUT=1` and `node --import tsx --input-type=module`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the real MCP loopback server, issued a scoped non-owner bearer token for `agent:main:matrix:dm:issued` with `inboundEventKind=user_request`, then called `tools/list` while sending spoofed headers for `agent:main:telegram:group:spoofed`, `accountId=spoofed-account`, `messageProvider=telegram`, `inboundEventKind=room_event`, and `x-openclaw-sender-is-owner=true`.

Evidence after fix:
```text
[mcp-loopback] request {"batchSize":1,"methods":["tools/list"],"sessionKey":"agent:main:matrix:dm:issued","inboundEventKind":"user_request","senderIsOwner":false,"toolCount":20,"cronVisible":false}
[mcp-loopback] response {"method":"tools/list","isError":false}
```

Observed result after fix: The request succeeded, but the runtime log shows the server ignored the spoofed scope/owner headers and resolved `sessionKey=agent:main:matrix:dm:issued`, `inboundEventKind=user_request`, and `senderIsOwner=false` from the scoped token. The rebased revision additionally covers the new context fields with `src/gateway/mcp-http.test.ts` regressions ("does not let spoofed scope headers change resolved loopback context", "ignores spoofed owner and scope headers when the bearer token is non-owner scoped", and the scoped-token context-propagation test asserting all ten fields reach `resolveGatewayScopedTools`).

What was not tested: No known gaps.

## Test plan (run on the rebased head)
- pnpm test src/gateway/mcp-http.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts (146 passed)
- pnpm test src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts (passed)
- pnpm tsgo:core && pnpm tsgo:core:test
- node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs <changed files> (clean)
- pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <changed files> (clean)

target.title: feat(tasks): improve task state transitions and lifecycle formatting
target.body:
## Summary
- add explicit intermediate active task states for approval waits and external waits
- propagate those active-state semantics across registry maintenance, flow guards, CLI views, and auto-reply task rendering
- add compressed operational summaries plus shared task lifecycle event formatting for state-change updates
- reuse the shared lifecycle formatter in task update delivery paths and extend focused task coverage

## Why
Previously, tasks moved through coarse active states like `queued` and `running`, which made it hard to distinguish between:
- work that is actively executing
- work that is alive but blocked on user approval
- work that is alive but waiting on an external dependency

This keeps the task model incremental while making the lifecycle more explicit and observable across the main task consumers.

## Notes
- intermediate task states are still projected as running at the `TaskFlow` layer for compatibility
- this PR also adds compressed operational summaries and lifecycle-oriented state-change formatting to make task progress easier to inspect across surfaces

## Testing
- OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test:fast -- src/tasks/task-status.test.ts src/tasks/task-executor-policy.test.ts
- OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test:fast -- src/tasks/task-status.test.ts src/tasks/task-executor-policy.test.ts src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts src/tasks/task-registry.store.test.ts
- existing focused task-state tests retained in the PR history for intermediate active state semantics
